title: Carbonnade flamande
subtitle: Flemish beef and beer stew with caramelised onions
excerpt: Thick slices of braising beef cooked low and slow in Belgian ale with a large quantity of caramelised onions, a spoonful of mustard, and a slice of bread spread with more mustard laid across the top to thicken the stew as it bakes. A robust, sweet-and-bitter winter dish.
sourceNotes: Carbonnade flamande (also carbonade) is documented in nineteenth-century Belgian and northern French cookery as a working-class braise using local dark ale rather than wine. The mustard-bread lid is a traditional thickener documented across multiple Flemish sources. The dish is closely associated with the Ghent region.

content.text: The carbonnade is the Flemish braise: made with ale rather than wine, sweetened with a large amount of slowly cooked onion, and finished with a slice of mustard-spread bread that dissolves into the top of the stew and thickens the sauce. It is a simple dish that rewards patience at the onion stage. The onions need at least 20 minutes over low heat to become properly golden and sweet; rushed, they contribute bitterness rather than depth.

Use a dark, malty Belgian ale or a stout. The bitterness of the hops balances the sweetness of the onions. Avoid very hoppy IPAs, which leave an unpleasant metallic edge in a long braise. Braising steak or beef chuck both work; shin is better still if you can get it in chunks rather than slices.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Season the {{beef-chuck}} of beef slices generously. Heat the olive oil in a casserole over high heat. Brown the beef in batches, 3 minutes per side, until deeply coloured. Do not crowd the pan. Remove and set aside.

Lower the heat to medium-low and add the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. Add the {{onion}} and a pinch of salt and cook, stirring occasionally, for 20 to 25 minutes until deeply golden and sweet. Add the {{garlic}} and cook for 2 minutes more. Stir in 1 tablespoon of the Dijon mustard.

Return the beef to the casserole, layering it with the onions. Pour in the {{beer}} of ale and {{stock-beef}} of beef stock. Add the {{thyme}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Spread the remaining tablespoon of Dijon mustard thickly over the bread slices and lay them mustard-side down on top of the stew. Bring to a simmer, cover, and transfer to the oven. Cook for 2 hours 30 minutes, then remove the lid for the final 20 minutes to brown the bread crust. The bread should dissolve into the sauce. Remove the herb sprigs and serve.

items.fix: Stir the bread crust into the sauce; if still thin, simmer uncovered for 10 minutes on the hob after removing the beef
items.cause: Bread did not dissolve fully, or too much liquid added

items.fix: Stir in a small spoonful of sugar or a knob of butter; use a dark malty ale (Leffe Brune, Chimay) next time, not a hop-forward IPA
items.cause: Ale was too hoppy, or onions not cooked long enough

items.fix: Return to the oven for another 30 minutes; check the temperature, the braise should be barely simmering, not boiling
items.cause: Cut too thick, or oven too hot
items.symptom: Beef is chewy at the 2.5-hour mark

items.fix: Deglaze with a splash of water, scrape the base, and continue over low heat; caught onions taste bitter and will spoil the sauce
items.cause: Heat too high or not enough stirring

content.text: The carbonnade comes from Flanders, the French-speaking south of Belgium and the border regions of northern France where cattle farming and brewing overlapped for centuries. The logic of the dish is simple: where there is no wine, braise in beer. The result is richer and more complex than the logic suggests. It is the dish of Belgian brasseries from autumn through winter, served with frites or mashed potato, and it appears on the table of every Flemish family at least once between October and March.
